text: The surgeon introduces a commercially available endo bag on the right side, enclosing the entire ovary with a 6.5 cm dermoid cyst inside the bag to prevent contamination during aspiration or spillage.

The surgeon uses a harmonic device to puncture the ovary, creating a small opening for a 5mm suction device to aspirate the fluid content of the dermoid cyst. As the cyst partially collapses, it becomes easier to grasp and strip the outer ovarian cortex from the inner cystic layer.

The surgeon uses ambidextrous technique to strip the outer ovarian cortex from the inner cystic layer of the dermoid cyst. The left hand holds the outer ovarian cortex while the right hand strips the cystic layer, achieving symmetrical enucleation. A Maryland dissector and a grasper are used to facilitate the stripping, revealing a good plane for easy separation of the dermoid cyst.

Approximately fifty percent of the cyst is visible outside the ovary, with the remaining portion being carefully separated. The fimbria and tube are protected to avoid damage, as these patients often have infertility concerns. Seventy-five percent of the cyst is now out, with only twenty-five percent still attached. The cyst continues to be extracted, revealing a large hairball inside. Ninety percent of the cyst is now out, with just a small attachment remaining. The cyst is fully removed and carefully placed into the endo bag using jerky movements to ensure it reaches the base without further spillage.

The surgeon maneuvers the endo bag to the base, ensuring controlled spillage of the dermoid cyst contents. The endo bag is repositioned to the left side, where the left ovary is placed inside for cystectomy. The outer ovarian cortex of the left ovary is carefully cut, revealing the small 1.8 mm dermoid cyst. The cyst is gently dissected without rupture, using a grasper instead of direct holding with a Maryland, to prevent rupture. The base of the cyst is visible as the ovary is stripped out.

The surgeon confirms the dermoid cyst is intact and fully enclosed within the endo bag. Using a 10 mm port, the mouth of the endo bag is externalized. Ovum forceps are used to rupture the cyst and remove its contents, including hair, one by one. A semi-traumatic grasper holds both sleeves of the endo bag, which is inserted into the cannula. The cannula is also removed together with the endo bag. The rectus is occasionally stretched with long jaw artery forceps as the hair ball is gradually extracted, completing the surgery.
